lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market mills that repeatedly weave broadwoven fabrics or felts for external business customers, including mills that also finish or further fabricate their woven output.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive weaving operations, non-control financing vehicles, shell entities, rug and tire-fabric mills, standalone finishing mills, and firms without recurring external-customer supply relationships.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat B2B sale of fabric by order, specification, yardage, or production run, with some contract/jobber arrangements and customer-specific finishing or fabrication.

a.bridge: BLS measures the broader 3132 Fabric Mills occupation population, not 313210 wage-weighted AI task exposure. Fabric Mills employed 8,310 textile knitting/weaving operators and 4,280 winding/twisting operators in May 2024; these physical machine roles dominate the visible mix. I infer a smaller exposed share concentrated in production planning, purchasing, quoting, documentation, maintenance triage, computer vision inspection support, and back office. Aggregation to 3132 and omission of occupation wages could overstate or understate 313210 exposure, while treating machine automation as AI would bias upward, so it is excluded.
a.rationale: Broadwoven production is equipment- and operator-intensive. Current generative and analytical AI can remove portions of information work and assist quality control, but cannot directly replace most loom tending, material handling, setup, repair, or physical inspection accountability within five years.
a.change_evidence: A 313210 payroll extract mapping paid hours and wages to tasks, combined with time-and-motion studies for quoting, scheduling, inspection, maintenance, purchasing, and administrative workflows at at least ten mills.
caveats: OEWS publishes the relevant occupation evidence at 3132 rather than 313210.
The estimate excludes conventional loom, sensor, and robotics automation already embodied in current operations.
Census AI adoption evidence covers all US businesses, not textile mills specifically.

rho.rationale: Within the limited exposed task pool, standardized office workflows and decision support should be implementable, but mill-specific ERP data, legacy controls, sample variation, customer specifications, false-defect costs, cybersecurity, and scarce technical staff slow deployment. Census found economy-wide AI use only 3.7% to 5.4% from September 2023 to February 2024 and noted workflow and training changes among adopters, supporting a measured implementation rate rather than near-full capture.
rho.change_evidence: Five-year implementation records from textile-mill ERP, MES, machine-vision, and predictive-maintenance vendors, including go-live rates, measured hours saved, false-reject rates, and abandonment rates for 313210 customers.
caveats: No source directly measures five-year AI implementation in broadwoven mills.
Small LMM mills may have less clean data and integration capacity than the average adopter.

e.rationale: The NAICS definition is inherently external-output manufacturing and permits weaving-only through weaving, finishing, and further fabrication. Many mills therefore support repeat B2B supply relationships, but the dataset firm count can include captive plants, highly concentrated proprietary producers, distressed assets, and firms whose earnings band or customer model does not survive diligence.
e.change_evidence: A complete 313210 establishment-to-firm roster enriched with ownership, customer concentration, captive status, normalized EBITDA, and the share of revenue from repeat external orders.
caveats: Eligibility is estimated against the frozen n=77 rather than observed firm-level screening.
SUSB-derived firm counts and NAICS establishments need not map one-to-one.

s5.bridge: A Census study of women-owned employer businesses reports manufacturing repeatedly among sectors with high shares of owners aged 55 or more across race and ethnicity groups, with reported manufacturing values ranging from 44.4% to 63.1% in several groups. I translate that succession pressure into a bounded 3%-6% annual qualifying-control-transfer hazard and compound it over five years. Owner age is not a transfer, the study is not 313210-specific, and it excludes male-owned firms; these differences can bias either way, while excluding closures and family/internal transfers reduces the qualifying probability.
s5.rationale: Capital-intensive mills can remain owner-operated late in the owner's career, creating succession pressure, but specialized equipment, cyclicality, environmental liabilities, customer concentration, and family succession can prevent an external control transaction.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, GF Data, BizBuySell, Axial, and broker transaction records tagged to 313210, linked to a firm roster and owner-age data, with closures, minority investments, internal reorganizations, and non-operating asset sales removed.
caveats: The source population is women-owned manufacturing across broad demographic groups, not all broadwoven mills.
Aging-owner prevalence does not measure completion of a transferable control sale.

q.rationale: Fabric is generally priced per order or physical unit rather than as transparent labor cost-plus billing, so labor savings can initially remain in mill margin. Retention erodes as contracts renew, large apparel and industrial buyers rebid supply, imports discipline price, and competitors adopt similar scheduling, inspection, and administrative tools. The 313210 PPI moved only narrowly from 164.106 in May 2024 to 163.568 in September 2024, consistent with limited short-run price expansion but not a direct pass-through measure.
q.change_evidence: Customer contracts and invoice-level gross-margin cohorts before and after documented automation, split by fixed-price, index-linked, toll-conversion, and bid-based arrangements, plus renewal and win/loss data.
caveats: PPI measures output prices, not the share of an AI benefit retained.
Customer bargaining power and import exposure vary materially by fabric specification and end market.

d5.bridge: Federal Reserve industrial production for the broader 3132 Fabric Mills population fell from 89.8390 in 2021 to 75.8120 in 2025, while 2023-2025 was roughly flat to slightly higher (74.6653, 75.6519, 75.8120). I combine the structural decline with recent stabilization to estimate 313210 constant-quality quantity five years forward. Broader-industry aggregation and the production index's use of production-worker hours can misstate true 313210 demand; hours-based measurement may bias downward when productivity rises.
d5.rationale: Domestic broadwoven demand is durable as an intermediate input for apparel, furnishings, and industrial uses, but US mill quantity faces import substitution, customer relocation, material substitution, and long-run productivity and consolidation pressure. Recent 3132 output stabilization prevents simply extrapolating the steep 2021-2023 fall.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year customer-volume forecasts and booked programs by fabric type, USITC import penetration and square-yard shipment data, and 313210-specific Federal Reserve or Census real shipment series adjusted for mix and quality.
caveats: The Federal Reserve notes that since 2022 textile detail has relied on production-worker hours, which is an imperfect output proxy.

o.rationale: The year-five service basket is the physical weaving, finishing, and fabrication of fabric. Design software, procurement platforms, and AI planning can reduce labor or enable some self-service ordering, but customers still require an accountable mill to own equipment, handle materials, meet specifications, inspect quality, and deliver physical output.
o.change_evidence: Customer interviews covering reshoring, vertical integration, direct digital procurement, additive or nonwoven material substitution, and the portion of planned 2031 fabric volume that could bypass a broadwoven mill operator.
caveats: Operator requirement is estimated rather than directly surveyed.
Material substitution to nonwovens, knits, composites, or imported finished goods belongs partly in demand and could also reduce the operator-required share.

narrative.executive_view: Broadwoven mills sell a necessary physical intermediate through repeat B2B relationships, but the near-term AI opportunity is mainly around the plant rather than at the loom. The opportunity depends on disciplined workflow deployment in planning, quoting, quality support, maintenance, purchasing, and administration while preserving accountable physical operations.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can structure specifications and orders, forecast schedules and material needs, assist root-cause and maintenance analysis, triage visual defects, draft compliance documentation, and automate routine customer and back-office work. Most operator, setup, material-handling, repair, and final quality-accountability tasks remain physical and are not counted as substitutable simply because conventional machinery can automate them.
narrative.value_capture: Unit- or order-priced fabric lets an early operator retain some labor and throughput benefit, but powerful buyers, competitive bids, import alternatives, and renewal repricing should share much of it over five years. Savings that improve lead time, quality consistency, or small-batch economics may retain better than generic office efficiencies.
narrative.firm_availability: The frozen dataset estimates 77 firms in the EBITDA band, but only an estimated subset has repeat external-customer revenue, independent control, defensible normalized earnings, and transferable operations. Manufacturing owner-age evidence indicates succession pressure, though age is a weak proxy for a completed qualifying sale.
narrative.demand_durability: Fabric remains a physical input and therefore needs an accountable operator, but domestic output is exposed to imports, offshore customer production, alternative textile structures, and consolidation. Broader Fabric Mills production fell sharply after 2021 but stabilized around 2023-2025, supporting a declining rather than collapsing five-year base case.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest uncertainties are the lack of 313210-specific occupation and AI implementation data, reliance on broader 3132 production, heterogeneous end markets, and unobserved firm-level transferability. Legacy equipment integration, mill data quality, customer concentration, environmental or safety liabilities, and false quality decisions could erase implementation gains.
narrative.principal_driver: Repeat physical B2B demand with opportunities to improve information-heavy workflows without displacing the accountable mill operator.
narrative.principal_weakness: Most labor is tied to physical production while domestic fabric output faces structural import and substitution pressure.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 313210 Broadwoven Fabric Mills
sources.supports: Defines 313210 as establishments weaving broadwoven fabrics and felts, including weaving-only and mills that also finish or further fabricate products.

sources.title: May 2024 OEWS occupation-industry chart data
sources.supports: Reports 8,310 textile knitting and weaving machine operators and 4,280 textile winding, twisting, and drawing-out operators employed in Fabric Mills.

sources.title: BLS Occupational projections and worker characteristics
sources.supports: Reports projected 2024-2034 declines of 11.2% for textile knitting and weaving machine operators and 10.2% for textile machine operators overall, contextualizing an already-automated physical workforce.

sources.title: Tracking Firm Use of AI in Real Time: A Snapshot from the Business Trends and Outlook Survey
sources.supports: Reports economy-wide business AI use rising from 3.7% to 5.4% from September 2023 to February 2024, expected use near 6.6% by early fall 2024, and workflow and training changes among adopters.

sources.title: The Metamorphosis of Women Business Owners: A Focus on Age
sources.supports: Census 2021 ABS analysis shows manufacturing among sectors with high shares of owners age 55 and over in multiple race and ethnicity groups, including reported values from 44.4% to 63.1%.

sources.title: September 2024 Producer Price Index Detailed Report
sources.supports: Reports the 313210 broadwoven fabric mill output price index at 164.106 in May 2024 and 163.568 in September 2024, with product-level detail.

sources.title: Industrial Production: Fabric Mills (NAICS 3132)
sources.supports: Federal Reserve series reports annual 3132 industrial production of 89.8390 in 2021, 81.7985 in 2022, 74.6653 in 2023, 75.6519 in 2024, and 75.8120 in 2025.
